note: First stage — no standings yet. The first jerseys will be decided in today's team time trial in Barcelona.

days.headline: Punchy Barcelona finale — too hard for the pure sprinters, perfect for fast classics riders.

picks.why: Short, sharp finishing climbs are exactly his terrain, and he'll be targeting an early yellow jersey.
Fast enough to win a reduced sprint if the puncheurs' attacks are pulled back.
Cheaper than the stars and this is the kind of stage he lives for.

days.headline: First summit finish at Les Angles (3,940 m of climbing) — the GC race starts today.

picks.why: The default pick for any summit finish; also likely to be chasing every time bonus.
The other podium favourite — top-5 stage points are close to guaranteed on this terrain.
Value climber pick — free role if Evenepoel marks the favourites.

days.headline: Rolling Ariège roads to Foix the day after a summit finish — classic breakaway territory.

picks.why: Wildcard-team riders have to hunt stages like this one.
Climbs well enough to survive the Pyrenean foothills and often rides ahead of the GC group.
Cheap engine for a big escape day — EF always races aggressively.

days.headline: Flat run into Pau — the sprinters' first clear chance in days, so their teams will control it.

picks.why: Arguably the fastest man in the race on a straightforward finish.
Best leadout in the peloton and scores consistently even when he doesn't win.
Podium-regular sprinter who also survives harder finishes — great points-per-euro.

days.headline: The queen Pyrenean stage to Gavarnie-Gèdre — 4,149 m of climbing, biggest points day of the window.

picks.why: Long, high mountain days are where he's historically strongest.
If the race is close after Les Angles he'll attack here.
Likely to hunt the breakaway and mountain points if he's lost time on GC.

days.headline: Pan-flat run to Bordeaux (only 701 m of climbing all day) — a nailed-on bunch sprint.

picks.why: The most conventional sprint finish of the week suits him perfectly.
Bordeaux's long finishing straights reward a big leadout train.
Reliable top-5 sprinter who keeps scoring on days the pure flyers fade.

fantasy.strategy: This first transfer window (stages 1-7) is bookended by two serious Pyrenean summit finishes with sprint chances in between, so a balanced squad beats a specialist one: two or three sprinters earn their keep on stages 5 and 7, but make sure both marquee GC riders are aboard before stage 3. Remember climbing stages outscore sprint stages in this competition, so if you're picking a bonus (double-points) stage early, stage 6 is the biggest single pot of the week — take it if your team is climber-heavy, or bank a safer double on stage 7's near-certain bunch sprint if you're sprinter-stacked. Spend the transfer window's final moves converting a sprinter into a climber before the window shuts after stage 7.
